one_line_takeaway: MAGE repurposes agentic memory as a security primitive -- a shadow-stack-inspired 'shadow memory' plus a jointly RL-trained memory-manager/judge pair -- cutting attack success rates on long-horizon tool-chaining (STAC, 100%->8.3% ASR) and persistent indirect prompt injection (PI2, ->0.0% ASR across three backbones) with only single-digit-percent utility loss and lower token overhead than comparable baselines (MELON, IPIGuard).
key_finding: In Eval I (STAC, target agent GPT-5.1, MAGE backbone Qwen3-4B): No Defense gives 100.0% ASR; the untrained judge-only baseline (J-only, Qwen3-4B) gives 62.5% ASR; upgrading J-only to GPT-5-mini only reduces this to 37.5%; an RL-trained J-only variant reaches 16.7% ASR; full MAGE (J+M, Qwen3-4B, RL-trained) reduces ASR to 8.3% while preserving 94.4% Benign Utility at 7.0K extra tokens/task, versus 12.9K tokens for the RL-trained J-only variant (a 1.8x reduction). Untrained J+M variants collapse to 0.0% ASR but also 0.0% BU (they reject everything). Ablating only M (training J alone) gives 12.5% ASR / 88.9% BU, worse than joint training. Detection latency averages -0.06 turns (detection at or slightly before the attack turn). Removing the attack-pattern reference from shadow memory raises ASR from 8.3% to 20.8% (BU 88.9%); removing the user-intent reference raises ASR to 12.5% but drops BU more sharply, to 83.3%. Under adaptive (defense-aware) attacks, MAGE holds 8.3% ASR under M-adaptive and J-adaptive attacks individually, rising modestly to 12.5% under the combined J/M-adaptive attack, versus an untrained J+M baseline that degrades to 25.0-37.5% ASR and an RL-trained J-only baseline that degrades to 20.8-25.0% ASR. In Eval II (indirect prompt injection, AgentDojo Banking+Slack, MAGE backbone Qwen3-4B): MAGE achieves 0.0% ASR against PI2 (the long-horizon attack) on all three target agents (GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen3-235B), with average-across-5-attacks ASR of 0.7% (GPT-4o-mini), 0.4% (Llama-3.3), 0.0% (Qwen3-235B); corresponding Benign Utility (near the no-defense ceiling) is 59.5% for GPT-4o-mini (vs. 62.2% no-defense), 73.0% for Llama-3.3 (vs. 75.7% no-defense), and 86.5% for Qwen3-235B (equal to its 86.5% no-defense BU). Baseline defenses trade off worse: Spotlighting and Repeated-Prompt exceed 70% ASR against PI2; MELON reaches 0.7% ASR on GPT-4o-mini but degrades to 42.1%/20.7% average ASR on Llama-3.3/Qwen3; IPIGuard matches MAGE's attack robustness but at higher utility loss (5.4-8.1 percentage points of BU) and higher token overhead (e.g., 6.8K vs. MAGE's 2.9K average tokens on GPT-4o-mini); PI-Detector causes catastrophic utility loss (BU below 30%). Detection latency in Eval II averages 0.05 turns. Removing the user-intent memory component raises ASR from 0.7% to 7.5% and drops BU from 59.5% to 51.4%; removing the attack-pattern component has a smaller effect (ASR to 1.4%, BU roughly stable at 56.8%). Under adaptive PI2 attacks with full knowledge of MAGE, MAGE holds 0.0% ASR across M-adaptive, J-adaptive, and combined attacks, while an RL-trained J-only variant degrades from 8.9% (default/M-adaptive) to 21.4-23.2% ASR under J-adaptive/combined attacks.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MAGE (Memory As Guardrail Enforcement), a defensive framework for LLM agents that counters 'long-horizon threats' -- attacks in which malicious intent emerges only at the trajectory level across many turns rather than within any single turn. MAGE's own contribution is: (1) 'shadow memory', a dedicated security-focused agentic memory (explicitly analogized to the 'shadow stack' abstraction from systems security) that runs in parallel with the agent's working memory, distilling and retaining security-critical context (user intent + known attack patterns) while discarding irrelevant detail as the trajectory unfolds; (2) a two-component architecture -- a memory manager M that updates the shadow memory m_t = M(m_{t-1}, s_{t-1}), and a judge J that assesses each pending action against the current memory, d_t, e_t = J(a_t | m_t) -- both realized by a single shared small LLM (Qwen3-4B by default) invoked via native tool calls (update_memory, assess_risk) rather than special fine-tuned tokens; (3) a turn-wise variant of GRPO that assigns phase-separated, turn-level rewards: a binary correctness reward for the judge (Eq. 8), a temporally-discounted memory reward that propagates future judge outcomes backward through the memory-update chain with discount factor gamma (Eq. 9), plus format and length penalties, jointly optimizing detection accuracy, utility preservation, and token efficiency. At runtime, MAGE inserts a checkpoint at every turn: the agent's proposed action executes only if the judge approves it, otherwise it is blocked and the rationale reported to a human operator.
threat_model: The authors formalize a triadic user-agent-environment interaction (trajectory s_0, s_1, ... with s_i = <a_i, o_i, q_i>) and define a grey-box adversary that can observe the agent's execution trajectory (including benign user instructions under prompt injection) but cannot access the LLM's parameters or the deployed defense, and can adaptively refine its strategy across turns. Two adversary roles are studied: (a) user-as-adversary, where the adversary issues a sequence of malicious instructions {q*} to directly steer the agent, instantiated via Sequential Tool-Attack Chaining (STAC), a cited attack that chains individually-benign tool calls into a collectively malicious sequence; (b) environment-as-adversary, where the adversary embeds malicious payloads {o*} into environment observations (indirect prompt injection), instantiated via both short-horizon static attacks (InjecAgent, Prefix-Override, Tool-Knowledge, Important-Message, all cited from AgentDojo) and a long-horizon adaptive attack, Persistent Indirect Prompt Injection (PI2, also cited, from a companion paper by overlapping authors), which iteratively optimizes payloads adapted to context. The defender's goal is to detect adversarial prompts/injections before the malicious action a* executes while minimizing disruption to benign tasks, formalized as a per-turn judge d_t, e_t = J(a_t | tau_{t-1}). A separate 'adaptive attacker with full knowledge of MAGE' setting is also tested, where the attacker crafts prompts specifically to corrupt the memory manager (M-adaptive), bypass the judge via privilege-escalation injections (J-adaptive), or both (J/M-adaptive).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Conclusion/Future Work, Section 7): (1) the evaluation covers only two representative long-horizon attack classes (STAC-style tool-chaining and PI2-style indirect prompt injection); broader attack classes such as memory-poisoning attacks and 'goal drift', and other agent architectures such as coding or computer-use agents, are left to future work; (2) the shadow memory currently uses a fixed schema (tracking only user intent and known attack patterns); the authors flag dynamic, attack-adaptive schemas as an open direction; (3) the work is restricted to single-agent settings, and extending MAGE to multi-agent systems is stated as an open challenge. Additionally noted within the results themselves: RL training is essential -- untrained shadow-memory variants either reject nearly everything (catastrophic utility loss, e.g. 0.0% BU in Eval I, 5.4-8.1% BU in Eval II) or, if left unprotected against poisoning, uncritically absorb attacker-supplied 'pre-approved by security team' style claims into the memory as legitimate context (Example 1.2/2.2); combined M+J adaptive attacks still degrade MAGE somewhat in Eval I (ASR rises from 8.3% to 12.5%), showing the defense is not perfectly robust to a fully defense-aware adversary in that setting.

datasets_used: SHADE-Arena benchmark (cited; source of 247 malicious + 88 benign STAC-attack tasks for Eval I, plus 276 tasks for MAGE training)
AgentDojo benchmark, Banking and Slack task suites (cited; 43 benign + 14 injection tasks, 7,682 total test cases for Eval II)
quantitative_results: Eval I (STAC, target agent GPT-5.1): No Defense ASR 100.0%; MAGE (Qwen3-4B, RL-trained, J+M) ASR 8.3%, BU 94.4%, TC 7.0K tokens/task

Eval I ablation: J-only untrained Qwen3-4B ASR 62.5%/BU 79.2%; J-only untrained GPT-5-mini ASR 37.5%/BU 50.0%; J-only RL-trained Qwen3-4B ASR 16.7%/BU 77.8%/TC 12.9K; J+M untrained Qwen3-4B or GPT-5-mini ASR 0.0%/BU 0.0%; J+M with only J trained ASR 12.5%/BU 88.9%/TC 6.7K
Eval I attack-horizon sensitivity: No-Defense ASR rises from 89.7% (<=2 turns) to 100% (>=5 turns); MAGE holds ASR in 4.6%-10.0% across all horizons; mean detection latency -0.06 turns
Eval I shadow-memory ablation: full MAGE ASR 8.3%/BU 94.4%; w/o attack-pattern reference ASR 20.8%/BU 88.9%; w/o user-intent reference ASR 12.5%/BU 83.3%
Eval I adaptive attacks: MAGE ASR 8.3% (STAC/M-adaptive/J-adaptive), 12.5% (J/M-adaptive); untrained J+M baseline ASR 0.0%->25.0%/12.5%/37.5%; RL-trained J-only baseline ASR 16.7%->16.7%/20.8%/25.0%

Eval II (AgentDojo, averaged across 5 attacks incl. PI2), by target agent: GPT-4o-mini No Defense ASR 49.3%/BU 62.2% vs. MAGE ASR 0.7%/BU 59.5%/TC 2.9K; Llama-3.3-70B No Defense ASR 54.3%/BU 75.7% vs. MAGE ASR 0.4%/BU 73.0%/TC 3.0K; Qwen3-235B No Defense ASR 58.9%/BU 86.5% vs. MAGE ASR 0.0%/BU 86.5%/TC 3.5K
Eval II PI2-specific ASR: MAGE achieves 0.0% ASR against PI2 on all three backbones (vs. 98.2%/92.9%/92.9% No-Defense ASR on GPT-4o-mini/Llama-3.3/Qwen3-235B respectively)

Eval II baseline comparison (GPT-4o-mini backbone, average across 5 attacks): PI-Detector ASR 11.4%/BU 24.3%; Spotlighting ASR 46.1%/BU 59.4%; Repeated-Prompt ASR 25.4%/BU 64.9%; MELON ASR 0.7%/BU 56.8%/TC 3.2K; IPIGuard ASR 0.7%/BU 56.8%/TC 6.8K; MAGE ASR 0.7%/BU 59.5%/TC 2.9K
Eval II attack-horizon sensitivity (PI2, GPT-4o-mini backbone): No-Defense ASR exceeds 90% at >=3 attack turns; MAGE maintains near-zero ASR across horizons; mean detection latency 0.05 turns
Eval II shadow-memory ablation: full MAGE BU 59.5%/ASR 0.7%; w/o user-intent reference BU 51.4%/ASR 7.5%; w/o attack-pattern reference BU 56.8%/ASR 1.4%
Eval II adaptive PI2 attacks: MAGE ASR 0.0% across PI2/M-adaptive/J-adaptive/J-M-adaptive; untrained J+M baseline ASR 0.0%/0.0%/3.6%/3.6%; RL-trained J-only baseline ASR 8.9%/8.9%/21.4%/23.2%
baselines_compared: Eval I: ablated variants of MAGE only (J-only vs. J+M; trained vs. untrained; Qwen3-4B vs. GPT-5-mini backbone) -- no prior published defense specifically targets STAC-style tool-chaining attacks
Eval II: Spotlighting (cited), Repeated-Prompt (cited, from AgentDojo), PI-Detector (cited, from AgentDojo), MELON (cited), IPIGuard (cited)
